prompt: I want to add observability to my DSPy pipeline so I can see what prompts are being sent, token usage, and latency. I heard about Langfuse. Show me how to set it up.
expected_output: A working Langfuse + DSPy setup with DSPyInstrumentor, environment variable configuration, and a simple traced DSPy call.
assertions: Installs both langfuse and openinference-instrumentation-dspy packages
Sets LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY, LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY, and LANGFUSE_BASE_URL environment variables
Calls DSPyInstrumentor().instrument() before any DSPy module execution
Configures DSPy LM with provider-agnostic format and alternative comment
Mentions langfuse.flush() for scripts or short-lived processes

prompt: I already have Langfuse tracing working with DSPy. Now I want to add user IDs and session IDs to my traces so I can filter by customer in the dashboard. How?
expected_output: Code using @observe() decorator with propagate_attributes() to add user_id, session_id, tags, and metadata to auto-captured DSPy traces.
assertions: Uses propagate_attributes() context manager with user_id and session_id
Shows that propagate_attributes wraps the DSPy module call, not the other way around
Does not create a new get_client() instance inside every function

prompt: Should I use Langfuse or Arize Phoenix for my DSPy project? I want tracing and evaluation, and my team needs to review AI outputs for quality.
expected_output: A comparison recommending Langfuse for annotation queues and human review, Phoenix for local-first development, with a clear decision framework.
assertions: Mentions that both use the same OpenInference DSPy plugin for auto-instrumentation
Highlights Langfuse annotation queues as a differentiator for human review
Highlights Phoenix local mode as a differentiator for no-cloud development
Does not dismiss either tool -- presents tradeoffs
Mentions other alternatives like Langtrace and Weave
